en_title: The Geography Of Vietnam's Three Regions: Rivers: North Vietnam
en_body:
RIVERS OF VIETNAM: Vietnam has 11,500 km of waterways: the chief rivers of North Vietnam are the Hồng-Hà and Thái-Bình; Central Vietnam has only short rivers; South Vietnam has the most rivers, its principal streams the Cửu-Long and Đồng-Nai together with many lesser rivers. The river-system plays a most important role in Vietnam's economy: distributing water into the rice-fields and serving transportation.

North Vietnam: North Vietnam has 3,300 km of waterways. Its two principal rivers are the Hồng-Hà and Thái-Bình. The Hồng-Hà is also called the Cái River and Nhị-Hà River: 1,200 km long from Lào-Cai to the Gulf of North Vietnam (510 km). The Hồng-Hà rises in Vân-Nam at 2,000 m and flows Northwest/Southeast: at Yên-Bái the current turns fierce from streams and waterfalls pouring down the high mountains. The Hồng-Hà runs 510 km through Lào-Cai, Yên-Bái, Phú-Thọ, Sơn-Tây, Vinh Yên, Phúc-Yên, Bắc-Ninh, Hà-Nội, Hưng-Yên, Nam-Định and Thái-Bình.

 The Lô-Giang and Đà-Giang are two tributaries. The Lô-Giang is larger, rising in China and entering Vietnamese territory at Thanh-Thủy (the very river whose name the revolutionary Nhất Linh used as the title of his novel-cycle "The Thanh-Thủy River"). Two important tributaries of the Lô-Giang are the Gầm River flowing into Tuyên-Quang, and the Chảy River flowing into Đoan-Hùng, Phú-Thọ province. The Đà-Giang has its source in Vân-Nam. The river enters Vietnam through Lai-Châu, Chợ-Bờ, then turns its course upward to Hòa-Bình and returns to the Hồng River at Việt-Trì (yes, Việt-Trì bánh cuốn) in Phú-Thọ, there to meet the Lô-Giang.

 The Đáy River flowing into the Hồng-Hà at Việt-Trì forms the summit of North Vietnam's central delta triangle. At this important place of North Vietnam, the Hồng-Hà doubles its breadth from 500 to 1,000 meters, then enters the sea through four principal mouths: Trà-Lý, Ba-La, Lác and Đài.

The Thái-Bình River is formed where the Cầu, Thương, Lục-Nam, Đuống and Luộc Rivers meet at Kiếp-Bạc: from here it divides into the Bạch-Đằng, Hòn and Kinh-Môn Rivers; flowing to the sea through the mouths of Nam-Triệu, Cấm, Vạn-Ức and Thái-Bình. The Thái-Bình begins from Lục-Đầu-Giang at Kiếp-Bạc: here is the Bạch-Đằng River, a historic place in the national history of Vietnam—where Vietnam twice defeated Chinese invaders: Ngô-Quyền vanquished Crown Prince Hoằng-Thao of the Southern Hán army; Hưng-Đạo-Vương Trần Quốc Tuấn defeated Crown Prince Thoát-Hoan of the Nguyên dynasty.
note: Bạch-Đằng recalls two defining Vietnamese victories against Chinese invasion, led by Ngô Quyền in 938 and Trần Hưng Đạo in 1288.
